title: Lesson 11: AI Agents & Agentic Workflows Part 11

sections.content: Overview

Agentic workflows represent a fundamental shift in how autonomous systems are architected—moving from rigid sequential pipelines to adaptive, goal-driven loops where AI agents dynamically determine their own execution paths. Traditional orchestration systems require explicit branching logic, error handlers, and predetermined decision trees; they break down when faced with novel scenarios, unreliable tool outputs, or unanticipated state changes. In production systems like customer support automation, scientific discovery, and financial analysis, agentic workflows solve the brittleness problem by embedding reasoning into the execution layer itself. An agent observes its environment, evaluates its current state against desired goals, selects appropriate tools or actions, executes them, observes outcomes, and refines its strategy—all in a tightly coupled feedback loop. This is essential because real-world problems rarely have static solutions; a bank fraud detection agent must adapt when transaction patterns shift, a research agent must pivot when initial hypotheses fail, and a supply chain optimizer must replan when suppliers become unavailable. Without agentic loops, systems require constant manual intervention, rule rewrites, and deployment cycles. With them, agents can autonomously recover from failures, explore alternative strategies, and improve their decision-making through experience.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: In the 2019 World Cup final at Lord's, Ben Stokes faced an incredibly tense moment late in the innings with England's fate hanging in the balance—he couldn't just execute a pre-planned sequence of shots because the match conditions, field placements, and psychological pressure kept changing over by over. Stokes continuously observed the bowler's approach, assessed the field arrangement, identified gaps, executed a shot, watched how it played out, and immediately adjusted his strategy for the next delivery based on the outcome—he might switch from aggressive batting to defensive play, from targeting leg-side gaps to off-side opportunities. This is exactly how agentic workflows operate: the agent (Stokes) observes its environment (bowler, field, score), evaluates its goal (maximize runs, survive), selects actions (shot selection, running between wickets), executes them (actually batting), observes outcomes (field changes, boundary conditions), and refines strategy for the next iteration. The key insight is that Stokes didn't follow a rigid batting order determined before the match; instead, he reasoned in real-time, adapted to dynamic conditions, and made autonomous decisions that ultimately led to England's victory. This mirrors how agentic systems work: they succeed not through pre-scripted workflows but through continuous perception-decision-action loops that adapt to new information.
Core Concepts
Agent State & Goal Representation

Agent state encompasses all information an agent maintains about its current situation—observations from the environment, internal memory of past actions and outcomes, constraints it operates under, and metrics tracking progress toward objectives. Unlike monolithic systems with a single global state, agentic workflows partition state into agent-local representations that include explicit goal specifications, allowing the agent to reason about what needs to be accomplished and why. State representation is critical because it determines what patterns the agent can recognize and what decisions it can make; a customer service agent's state might include conversation history, customer satisfaction scores, available resolution options, and escalation thresholds, whereas a research agent's state includes discovered papers, experimental results, hypothesis validity scores, and research direction confidence. The goal representation must be unambiguous enough to guide decision-making but flexible enough to accommodate subgoals and revised approaches when initial plans fail. In production systems, state management accounts for significant complexity because agents often operate asynchronously across distributed systems; state must be durable, queryable, versioned, and recoverable from failures without losing critical context. The distinction between state and goal is especially important: state is what is true now (objective facts), while goals are what should become true (desired future states). A poorly designed state representation forces an agent into reactive behaviors only; a well-designed one enables strategic reasoning, long-horizon planning, and recovery from setbacks.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: During a Test match innings, a batsman like Virat Kohli maintains an internal state that includes the number of runs scored so far, wickets lost by the team, current partnerships, balls faced, dot balls in the recent over, and whether the pitch is deteriorating—alongside this state sits an explicit goal, such as "reach 100 runs by end of day" or "stabilize the innings after losing three wickets." Kohli's state updates with every delivery: he sees a short ball, registers it as a potential hook opportunity, recalls the fielder positions at deep square leg, evaluates his technique against the bowler's pattern, and executes accordingly. His goal might shift dynamically: early in the innings the goal is "build a foundation," in the middle overs "accelerate and score quickly," and in the final hour "bat for time and defend." The explicit goal representation lets him reason about trade-offs: should he play a risky shot to accelerate toward the 100-run target, or should he play defensively because losing his wicket would hurt the team's overall position? State representation includes everything he observes about the match (pitch behavior, field placement, bowler fatigue), while the goal guides how he prioritizes actions. This mirrors agentic systems perfectly: without clear goal representation, an AI agent cannot distinguish between making progress and spinning its wheels, and without detailed state representation, it cannot reason about complex, multi-step plans that adapt as conditions change.
Perception-Action Loops & Feedback

The perception-action loop is the core execution mechanism of agentic workflows: the agent observes its environment (perception), evaluates the current state against its goals (reasoning), selects an action (planning), executes it (action), and receives feedback (observation of new state). This creates a tight coupling between sensing and acting that is fundamentally different from batch-processing systems where all sensing happens upfront, then planning occurs, then actions execute in isolation. Feedback is the critical enabler of adaptation; without rapid, accurate feedback, an agent cannot recognize when its plans are failing and adjust course. Real-world feedback is often noisy, delayed, or incomplete: an agent might take an action that produces delayed effects (like sending an email that takes minutes to process), partial effects (a network request succeeds but returns incomplete data), or side effects (an action meant to solve problem A accidentally creates problem B). The challenge is designing feedback mechanisms that provide enough signal for the agent to learn and adapt without overwhelming the system with noise or creating infinite loops where the agent keeps trying the same failed action. In multi-agent systems, feedback from one agent's actions becomes perception for other agents, creating complex interdependencies that must be carefully managed to avoid deadlocks or divergent goals. Frequency of perception-action cycles also matters significantly: real-time agents like trading systems or robotics require sub-millisecond loops, while planning agents might operate on minute or hour scales. The feedback loop must also include explicit error detection and recovery: when an action fails or produces unexpected results, the agent needs mechanisms to understand why (exception details, partial results, side effects) and to recover gracefully rather than crashing.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider a fielder like Ravindra Jadeja in the field during a T20 match—he continuously cycles through perception and action at high speed. He perceives where the ball goes off the bat, predicts its trajectory based on pitch, wind, and batsman positioning (perception phase). He mentally evaluates his goal: "minimize runs, get a wicket if possible" against the current state: "ball in the air at mid-wicket, batsman is a left-hander who pulls," then decides to move two steps to his left toward deep mid-wicket (reasoning and planning). He moves into position and keeps his eyes on the ball (action). The ball lands in his hands, and he immediately perceives the new state: "ball caught, wicket down." He now adjusts his next action: celebrate with teammates and reset for the next delivery. If instead the ball had landed just beyond his reach, his feedback would be different: "miss, ball goes for four," and his reasoning for the next play would reflect this failure—perhaps moving more aggressively or positioning deeper. The tight perception-action-feedback loop happens continuously, every delivery; Jadeja doesn't think through an entire over's strategy upfront. This is exactly how agentic workflows operate: they cycle rapidly between observing outcomes, evaluating progress, and selecting next actions, with each cycle informed by the concrete feedback from the previous action. Without this tight loop, Jadeja would have to pre-plan where to stand for every possible delivery type, which would be impossible; with the loop, he adapts fluidly.
Tool Use & Capability Grounding

Agents accomplish complex goals by invoking external tools—API calls to retrieve data, database queries, machine learning models for classification, computational systems for simulation, or other agents for specialized subtasks. Tool use is the mechanism through which agents extend their capabilities beyond pure reasoning; a planning agent alone cannot actually book a flight, but when grounded with a booking system API, it becomes a travel assistant. Capability grounding is the process of an agent learning what tools are available, what they can and cannot do, their preconditions (what must be true before calling them), postconditions (what will be true after), their costs (latency, monetary, rate limits), and failure modes (what exceptions they throw, how they degrade). The challenge is that tool descriptions in production systems are often incomplete, inconsistent, or wrong; an API might change behavior after an update, or documentation might not reflect edge cases. Agents must therefore be robust to tool failures: they need mechanisms for fallback tools (if the primary booking API is down, try the backup), graceful degradation (if getting real-time pricing fails, use cached data), and error recovery (if a tool returns an error, determine if it's retryable or requires a different approach). Tool orchestration at scale involves managing dependencies between tools: some tools have prerequisites (you must authenticate before querying), some tools conflict (two tools might modify the same resource and cause inconsistency), and some tools have optimal ordering (it's faster to filter data at the database than to retrieve everything and filter in memory). The agent must also reason about tool costs: calling an expensive LLM model multiple times might yield better results than a single call, but might exceed budget or latency constraints.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket captain like Pat Cummins in an Australia Test match manages a team of specialist players—each player is a "tool" with specific capabilities grounded in their skills. Cummins has a world-class pace bowler (fast-bowling tool for breaking partnerships), a spin bowler (rotation tool for controlling run rate), a batsman who plays spin exceptionally (tool for building innings against spinners), a fielder with world-class reflexes (tool for catching), and others. Cummins must understand each tool's preconditions: the pace bowler works best when the pitch is hard and fresh, while the spinner works best when the pitch is cracked and worn. He must know postconditions: using the pace bowler aggressively might tire them for later in the match. He must know costs: pulling a specialist batsman into the field for a catch is beneficial (cost = removed from batting lineup later) or detrimental depending on match situation. He must have fallback plans: if the primary spinner gets injured, he substitutes with a part-time spinner or adjusts strategy. When the fast bowler fails (gets expensive or bowls badly), Cummins doesn't keep persisting; he removes them and rotates to a different tool. He reasons about dependencies: you cannot effectively rotate the strike without good batspeople in the crease, so the batting tool must be operational first. This mirrors agentic tool use exactly: agents must catalog available tools, understand their preconditions and postconditions, reason about costs and trade-offs, handle failures gracefully, and orchestrate tool invocations strategically to achieve goals. Poor tool grounding leads to agents making mistakes like invoking incompatible tools or persisting with broken ones; good grounding enables agents to adapt dynamically as conditions change.
Reasoning Strategies & Decision Making

Agentic reasoning encompasses the mechanisms by which agents select actions from their available options and decide whether to continue, pivot, or escalate when facing challenges. Different reasoning strategies suit different problem structures: reactive strategies (respond immediately to stimuli without lookahead) work for time-critical decisions like fraud detection, deliberative strategies (plan multiple steps ahead) work for complex goal achievement, and hierarchical strategies (decompose complex goals into subgoals) work for multi-phase problems. Chain-of-thought reasoning has emerged as a powerful pattern where agents reason explicitly about their steps before executing them, allowing them to catch errors, reconsider options, and explain their logic—this is particularly important for safety-critical systems and for building user trust. Exploration vs. exploitation trade-off arises in agents that learn from experience: should the agent try new strategies and tools (exploration) to potentially discover better approaches, or exploit known-good patterns (exploitation) to achieve reliable results quickly? In agentic workflows, this often manifests as retry strategies: when an action fails, should the agent retry with the same approach (might be transient failure), try a different tool (might work better), or escalate to a human (admit the task is out of scope)? Confidence thresholds are critical: an agent confident in its reasoning might act decisively, while low confidence might trigger uncertainty handling behaviors like requesting human input, running validation checks, or trying multiple approaches in parallel. Real-world agentic systems also incorporate reflection: agents should be able to evaluate their own decision-making process, recognize patterns in their failures, and update their reasoning strategies over time. Constraint satisfaction is often important: an agent might have multiple valid solutions to a problem but must choose one that respects constraints like cost, latency, risk tolerance, or regulatory compliance.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Jasprit Bumrah, one of the world's best pace bowlers, employs sophisticated reasoning strategies every delivery. Before each ball, he evaluates: what is the batter's weakness (leg-side early in the innings, yorkers at death)? What's my current execution confidence (high if I've practiced that delivery type recently)? Should I play it safe (predictable, reliable) or creative (unpredictable but riskier)? This is the exploration-exploitation trade-off: in early overs, Bumrah exploits his proven yorker with high execution confidence; in the powerplay, he might explore unconventional lines to test the batter's weaknesses. When a ball gets hit for four runs, Bumrah reflects: was it a bad decision (wrong delivery type), poor execution (planned a yorker but bowled a full toss), or opponent skill (batter read me perfectly)? Based on this reasoning, he adjusts the next delivery: maybe try a different line, maybe accept that this batter is strong against this approach and switch to a different tool (slower ball, bouncers). He maintains confidence thresholds: if he's been expensive (lots of runs conceded), he might become more conservative and stick to proven patterns; if he's been cheap, he might try bolder deliveries. Bumrah also considers constraints: in a T20 match, he has limited overs (total resource budget), must preserve his body (fatigue constraint), and must balance taking wickets with controlling runs (multiple simultaneous goals). His reasoning incorporates hierarchical strategy: first-level goal is "take wickets or control runs," second-level subgoals are "get a batter out LBW" or "bowl yorkers," third-level tactical decisions are "which specific yorker line against this batter." This mirrors agentic reasoning exactly: agents must select from multiple strategies, evaluate their confidence, handle failures through reflection and adjustment, manage trade-offs between competing objectives, and adjust tactics based on real-world outcomes.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Agentic Cricket Match Advisor System
Demonstrates: Agent State, Perception-Action Loops, Tool Use, Reasoning Strategies
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Dict, Optional, Tuple
import random


class MatchPhase(Enum):
    """Match phases with different strategy priorities."""
    POWERPLAY = "powerplay"       # Overs 1-6
    MIDDLE_OVERS = "middle_overs" # Overs 7-15
    DEATH_OVERS = "death_overs"   # Overs 16-20


class BowlerType(Enum):
    """Types of bowlers with different capabilities."""
    FAST = "fast_bowler"
    SPINNER = "spinner"
    ALL_ROUNDER = "all_rounder"


@dataclass
class CricketPlayer:
    """Represents a cricket player with performance metrics."""
    name: str
    role: str  # batsman, bowler, all_rounder
    current_runs: int = 0
    balls_faced: int = 0
    innings_count: int = 0
    bowling_overs: float = 0.0
    runs_conceded: int = 0
    wickets_taken: int = 0
    
    def strike_rate(self) -> float:
        """Calculate runs per 100 balls faced."""
        if self.balls_faced == 0:
            return 0.0
        return (self.current_runs / self.balls_faced) * 100
    
    def economy_rate(self) -> float:
        """Calculate runs per over bowled."""
        if self.bowling_overs == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.runs_conceded / self.bowling_overs


@dataclass
class MatchState:
    """Represents the current state of a cricket match (Agent State)."""
    match_id: str
    current_over: float  # e.g., 5.3 means 5 overs, 3 balls
    current_phase: MatchPhase
    runs_scored: int
    wickets_lost: int
    
    batting_team_name: str
    current_batsman: CricketPlayer
    current_bowler: CricketPlayer
    
    available_batsmen: List[CricketPlayer] = field(default_factory=list)
    available_bowlers: List[CricketPlayer] = field(default_factory=list)
    
    target_runs: int = 180  # What the batting team aims for
    max_overs: int = 20     # T20 format
    
    def update_phase(self):
        """Update match phase based on current over."""
        over_number = int(self.current_over)
        if over_number <= 6:
            self.current_phase = MatchPhase.POWERPLAY
        elif over_number <= 15:
            self.current_phase = MatchPhase.MIDDLE_OVERS
        else:
            self.current_phase = MatchPhase.DEATH_OVERS
    
    def overs_remaining(self) -> float:
        """Calculate overs left in the match."""
        return self.max_overs - self.current_over
    
    def run_rate_required(self) -> float:
        """Calculate runs per over needed to reach target."""
        overs_left = self.overs_remaining()
        if overs_left <= 0:
            return 0.0
        runs_needed = max(0, self.target_runs - self.runs_scored)
        return runs_needed / overs_left


class CricketAdvisorAgent:
    """
    Autonomous agent that advises on cricket strategy using:
    - Explicit state representation (MatchState)
    - Perception-action loops
    - Tool use (strategy selection functions)
    - Reasoning strategies (confidence-based decision making)
    """
    
    def __init__(self
sections.risk_tolerance:
float = 0.5):
        self.agent_name = agent_name
        self.risk_tolerance = risk_tolerance  # 0 = conservative, 1 = aggressive
        self.action_history: List[Dict] = []  # Feedback history
        self.strategy_confidence: Dict[str, float] = {
            "aggressive": 0.5,
            "defensive": 0.5,
            "balanced": 0.8
        }
    
    def select_batting_strategy(self
sections.state:
MatchState) -> str:
        """
        TOOL 1: Determine batting strategy (aggressive/defensive/balanced)
        Uses state to reason about best approach.
        """
        run_rate_needed = state.run_rate_required()
        current_run_rate = state.runs_scored / (state.current_over + 0.001)
        runs_behind = state.target_runs - state.runs_scored
        overs_left = state.overs_remaining()
        
        # Reasoning: Compare actual run rate to required run rate
        if current_run_rate < run_rate_needed * 0.8:
            # Behind required rate, confidence in aggressive strategy increases
            self.strategy_confidence["aggressive"] = min(
                1.0, 
                self.strategy_confidence["aggressive"] + 0.15
            )
            return "aggressive"
        elif current_run_rate > run_rate_needed * 1.3 and overs_left > 5:
            # Ahead of required rate, confidence in defensive strategy increases
            self.strategy_confidence["defensive"] = min(
                1.0,
                self.strategy_confidence["defensive"] + 0.15
            )
            return "defensive"
        else:
            # On track, balanced strategy is safest
            return "balanced"
    
    def select_bowler(self

sections.match_phase:
MatchPhase) -> CricketPlayer:
        """
        TOOL 2: Select best bowler for current phase and batsman.
        Demonstrates tool selection with preconditions and postconditions.
        """
        if not state.available_bowlers:
            raise ValueError("No bowlers available")
        
        best_bowler = state.available_bowlers[0]
        best_score = -float('inf')
        
        for bowler in state.available_bowlers:
            # Precondition: bowler must have overs remaining
            if bowler.bowling_overs >= 4.0:  # Max 4 overs in T20
                continue
            
            score = 0
            
            # Phase-specific reasoning
            if match_phase == MatchPhase.POWERPLAY:
                # Powerplay: prefer fast bowlers with low economy
                if bowler.role == BowlerType.FAST.value:
                    score += 30
                score -= bowler.economy_rate() * 5  # Penalize expensive bowlers
            
            elif match_phase == MatchPhase.DEATH_OVERS:
                # Death overs: prefer experienced bowlers (high wickets taken)
                score += bowler.wickets_taken * 20
                score -= bowler.economy_rate() * 8  # Even more penalty for expensive bowling
            
            else:  # MIDDLE_OVERS
                # Middle overs: balanced approach
                score += bowler.wickets_taken * 10
                score -= bowler.economy_rate() * 6
            
            if score > best_score:
                best_score = score
                best_bowler = bowler
        
        return best_bowler
    
    def evaluate_confidence(self

sections.state:
MatchState) -> float:
        """
        TOOL 3: Evaluate agent's confidence in its current strategy.
        Confidence determines how boldly to recommend actions.
        """
        confidence = 0.0
        
        # Factor 1: Match situation clarity (0-0.3)
        run_rate_diff = abs(
            (state.runs_scored / (state.current_over + 0.001)) - 
            state.run_rate_required()
        )
        if run_rate_diff < 1.0:
            confidence += 0.3  # Clear situation
        elif run_rate_diff < 3.0:
            confidence += 0.15
        
        # Factor 2: Strategy consistency (0-0.3)
        avg_strategy_confidence = sum(
            self.strategy_confidence.values()
        ) / len(self.strategy_confidence)
        confidence += avg_strategy_confidence * 0.3
        
        # Factor 3: Wickets in hand (0-0.4)
        wickets_in_hand = 10 - state.wickets_lost
        if wickets_in_hand >= 5:
            confidence += 0.4
        elif wickets_in_hand >= 3:
            confidence += 0.2
        else:
            confidence += 0.05
        
        return min(1.0, confidence)
    
    def execute_perception_action_loop(self

MatchState) -> Dict:
        """
        CORE LOOP: Perception -> Reasoning -> Action
        This is the main agentic workflow.
        """
        print(f"\n[{self.agent_name}] Perception-Action Loop - Over {state.current_over}")
        print(f"  Current State: {state.runs_scored}/{state.target_runs} "
              f"({state.wickets_lost} wickets

phase:
Observe current state and update phase
        state.update_phase()
        
        # REASONING: Select strategy, bowler, and evaluate confidence
        batting_strategy = self.select_batting_strategy(state)
        best_bowler = self.select_bowler(state, state.current_phase)
        confidence = self.evaluate_confidence(state)
        
        print(f"  → Selected Strategy: {batting_strategy} (confidence: {confidence:.2f})")
        print(f"  → Selected Bowler: {best_bowler.name} (economy: {best_bowler.economy_rate():.2f})")
        
        # ACTION: Simulate action outcome based on strategy and bowler selection
        action_outcome = self._simulate_action(
            state, batting_strategy, best_bowler, confidence
        )
        
        # FEEDBACK: Record outcome for reflection
        self.action_history.append({
            "over": state.current_over,
            "strategy": batting_strategy,
            "bowler": best_bowler.name,
            "confidence": confidence,
            "outcome": action_outcome
        })
        
        return {
            "strategy": batting_strategy,
            "bowler_selected": best_bowler.name,
            "confidence": confidence,
            "outcome": action_outcome
        }
    
    def _simulate_action(self
phase.confidence:
float) -> Dict:
        """
        Simulate the outcome of strategy execution.
        In real systems, this would be actual execution with real feedback.
        """
        base_runs = random.randint(2, 8)
        bowler_effectiveness = 1.0 - (bowler.economy_rate() / 15.0)  # Normalize economy
        confidence_multiplier = 0.8 + (confidence * 0.4)  # Confidence affects results
        
        if strategy == "aggressive":
            runs_this_ball = int(base_runs * 1.3 * confidence_multiplier)
            wicket_probability = 0.15  # Higher risk
        elif strategy == "defensive":
            runs_this_ball = int(base_runs * 0.7 * confidence_multiplier)
            wicket_probability = 0.05  # Lower risk
        else:  # balanced
            runs_this_ball = int(base_runs * 1.0 * confidence_multiplier)
            wicket_probability = 0.1
        
        # Apply bowler effectiveness
        runs_this_ball = max(0, int(runs_this_ball * bowler_effectiveness))
        wicket_probability *= (1.0 - bowler_effectiveness)
        
        # Outcome
        is_wicket = random.random() < wicket_probability
        
        return {
            "runs_scored": runs_this_ball,
            "wicket": is_wicket,
            "bowler_effectiveness": bowler_effectiveness
        }


def demonstrate_agentic_workflow():
    """
    Main demonstration: Show complete agentic workflow in action.
    """
    print("="*70)
    print("CRICKET MATCH AGENTIC ADVISOR SYSTEM")
    print("Demonstrating: State, Perception-Action Loops, Tool Use, Reasoning")
    print("="*70)
    
    # Initialize players
    batting_team = [
        CricketPlayer("Rohit Sharma", "batsman"),
        CricketPlayer("Virat Kohli", "batsman"),
    ]
    
    bowling_team = [
        CricketPlayer("Jasprit Bumrah", "bowler", bowling_overs=0.0),
        CricketPlayer("Hardik Pandya", "all_rounder", bowling_overs=0.0),
    ]
    bowling_team[0].role = BowlerType.FAST.value
    bowling_team[1].role = BowlerType.ALL_ROUNDER.value
    
    # Initialize match state
    match_state = MatchState(
        match_id="IND_vs_AUS_T20",
        current_over=0.0,
        current_phase=MatchPhase.POWERPLAY,
        runs_scored=0,
        wickets_lost=0,
        batting_team_name="India",
        current_batsman=batting_team[0],
        current_bowler=bowling_team[0],
        available_batsmen=batting_team,
        available_bowlers=bowling_team,
        target_runs=180,
        max_overs=20
    )
    
    # Create agent
    advisor = CricketAdvisorAgent("Mumbai_Advisor_v1", risk_tolerance=0.6)
    
    # Simulate a few overs (perception-action loops)
    print(f"\nInitial Match State:")
    print(f"  Target: {match_state.target_runs} runs in {match_state.max_overs} overs")
    print(f"  Current: {match_state.runs_scored} runs, {match_state.wickets_lost} wickets out")
    
    simulated_overs = 5  # Run 5 overs
    for over_num in range(simulated_overs):
        match_state.current_over = over_num + 1.0  # Simplification: whole overs
        
        # Execute the perception-action loop
        result = advisor.execute_perception_action_loop(match_state)
        
        # Apply feedback: update match state
        match_state.runs_scored += result["outcome"]["runs_scored"]
        if result["outcome"]["wicket"]:
            match_state.wickets_lost += 1
            print(f"  → WICKET! Batting team now {match_state.wickets_lost} down")
        
        # Update bowler stats
        match_state.current_bowler.bowling_overs += 1.0
        match_state.current_bowler.runs_conceded += result["outcome"]["runs_scored"]
        
        print(f"  ✓ Match progresses to {match_state.runs_scored}/{match_state.target_runs} "
              f"({match_state.wickets_lost} wickets)")
    
    # Final summary
    print(f"\n" + "="*70)
    print("AGENT REFLECTION & LEARNING")
    print("="*70)
    print(f"\nTotal Actions Taken: {len(advisor.action_history)}")
    print(f"Strategy Confidence Scores (after learning):")
    for strategy, conf in advisor.strategy_confidence.items():
        print(f"  {strategy:12s}: {conf:.3f}")
    
    print(f"\nFinal Match State:")
    print(f"  Runs: {match_state.runs_scored}/{match_state.target_runs}")
    print(f"  Wickets: {match_state.wickets_lost}/10")
    print(f"  Run Rate Required: {match_state.run_rate_required():.2f} per over")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    demonstrate_agentic_workflow()

sections.content: The code above demonstrates all four core concepts working together in a complete agentic system. The `CricketAdvisorAgent` maintains explicit `MatchState` that captures runs scored, wickets lost, current over, phase (powerplay/middle/death), and goal (target of 180 runs). The `execute_perception_action_loop()` method is the core agent workflow: it first perceives the current state and updates the match phase, then calls three tools—`select_batting_strategy()` evaluates the gap between required and actual run rate and recommends aggressive/defensive/balanced play; `select_bowler()` uses phase-specific reasoning to select the best bowler with preconditions (must have overs remaining) and phase-aware scoring; `evaluate_confidence()` combines multiple factors (match clarity, strategy consistency, wickets in hand) to determine how boldly the agent should commit to its recommendations. After selecting a strategy and bowler, the agent executes a simulated action with `_simulate_action()`, which produces concrete feedback: runs scored and whether a wicket fell. This feedback is stored in `action_history` for later reflection, and the match state is updated to reflect the new reality. The `demonstrate_agentic_workflow()` function runs five perception-action cycles, showing the agent dynamically adjusting its strategy as the match progresses. Notice how the agent's strategy recommendations change based on run rate: if runs are behind target, confidence in aggressive strategy increases; if ahead, defensive strategy confidence increases. This mirrors how real-world agentic systems adapt their recommendations based on observable outcomes rather than executing a pre-planned sequence.
How It Works Under the Hood

Agentic workflows at runtime operate as asynchronous event-driven systems where perception, reasoning, and action are tightly coupled but may execute at different rates and on different hardware. In the perception phase, the agent gathers current state from multiple sources: sensor inputs, database queries, API responses, time-series observations, and output from previous actions. This data must be filtered for relevance and recency because stale observations can lead to poor decisions; a fraud detection agent operating on yesterday's transaction patterns might miss today's anomalies. The reasoning phase executes the agent's decision-making logic, which varies from simple rule-based evaluation (if X > threshold, do Y) to complex neural network inference (embedding current state, passing through transformer layers, generating probability distribution over actions). This phase is where latency bottlenecks often appear: if an agent's reasoning involves calling a language model, that inference adds 0.5-2 seconds of latency per decision; multiply by hundreds of concurrent agents and you're looking at thousands of GPUs. The action phase invokes external systems: API calls with retry logic, database transactions with rollback capability, model inferences with caching, or other agents with timeouts. Actions are rarely atomic; they often produce partial results (partial success), side effects (action A intended to do X but incidentally did Y), or asynchronous outcomes (action submitted but result comes later via webhook). Memory management is critical because agents often maintain context across hundreds or thousands of action cycles; in production systems, this long-form memory is typically stored in vector databases for semantic search (find similar past situations) or in graph databases for relational reasoning (this action conflicts with that constraint). The feedback loop closure—observing outcomes and updating state—is where learning happens; agents that can quickly recognize patterns in their feedback ("I tried aggressive strategy 10 times in powerplay and failed 7 of them") can adapt their future behavior. Error handling in the feedback phase is essential: if an action fails, the agent must determine the failure mode (transient network error vs. permanent precondition violation vs. unexpected side effect) and decide on recovery (retry, escalate, alternative action, or abort). In distributed agentic systems with multiple agents, state synchronization becomes complex; agents must agree on current state without constant communication, requiring mechanisms like vector clocks, eventual consistency models, or conflict resolution strategies.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A sports analytics team tracking Smriti Mandhana's batting performance during an ODI operates exactly like an agentic system's runtime mechanics. The perception phase happens continuously: cameras capture ball trajectory, pitch sensors detect bounce patterns, motion sensors track Mandhana's bat speed and body position, and crowd microphones pick up commentary (all real-time data flowing in). This raw data must be filtered: ignore noise from crowd, focus on relevant features like ball velocity, spin rate, line, and length. The reasoning phase computes within milliseconds: an AI system takes Mandhana's performance history (training data), her recent form (context), the current bowler's patterns (opponent model), pitch conditions, and game situation, then generates a probability distribution over outcomes (likely to play aggressive shot, defensive shot, leave the ball, etc.). If this reasoning requires consulting a neural network trained on thousands of ODI innings, that adds latency but improves accuracy. The action phase is Mandhana's actual batting: she swings the bat (action with execution uncertainty), makes contact or misses, ball travels toward fielders or boundary (partially uncertain outcomes). Feedback comes back instantly: if she hit it for four, the state updates (runs scored +4); if she got caught, state updates critically (wicket lost). Mandhana's long-form memory includes countless patterns she's learned: "when the pitch is green and pace bowlers are fresh, they bowl short; when it's worn, spinners dominate; when the run rate is slow, I should look for boundaries." She maintains these patterns implicitly but they guide her decision-making. Error handling happens automatically: if her first shot doesn't work as intended (she aimed at off-stump but played toward leg-stump), she observes the outcome and adjusts her next shot. In a multi-day Test series, she synchronizes her strategy with her team's overall state: if the team is batting badly and she's the last hope, her aggressive strategy adjusts to survival mode; if she's batting with a strong partner, she can take more risks. This mirrors agentic runtime: data flows in (perception), gets processed through decision models (reasoning), produces actions with uncertain outcomes, receives feedback that updates state, learns from patterns, handles failures gracefully, and synchronizes with other agents.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Advanced Agentic Workflow: Multi-Agent Cricket System with State Synchronization
Demonstrates: Runtime mechanics, async perception-action, error handling, learning loops
"""

import asyncio
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict, Optional, Tuple
from enum import Enum
import random


class ActionStatus(Enum):
    """Possible outcomes of actions."""
    SUCCESS = "success"
    PARTIAL_SUCCESS = "partial"
    FAILED = "failed"
    TIMEOUT = "timeout"
    SIDE_EFFECT = "side_effect"


@dataclass
class PerceptionData:
    """Data from environment sensors/APIs (Perception phase)."""
    timestamp: float
    current_over: float
    balls_in_over: int
    recent_deliveries: List[Dict]  # Last 6 balls
    current_batsman_mood: str  # "confident" or "cautious"
    pitch_condition: str  # "fresh", "worn", "deteriorating"
    bowler_fatigue: float  # 0-1, where 1 is completely fatigued
    wind_speed: float
    field_density: Dict[str, int]  # Position -> number of fielders


@dataclass
class ActionOutcome:
    """Result of an executed action."""
    action_type: str
    status: ActionStatus
    runs_produced: int
    wicket: bool
    execution_latency_ms: float
    confidence_delta: float  # How much this outcome affects strategy confidence
    side_effects: List[str] = None  # Unexpected consequences


class MultiAgentCricketSystem:
    """
    Advanced agentic system with multiple agents, state synchronization,
    async perception-action loops, and learning mechanisms.
    """

            ActionStatus.FAILED: "escalate_or_switch_strategy",
            ActionStatus.SIDE_EFFECT: "revert_if_critical"
        }

sections.content: Pro Tip: In production agentic systems, implement observability at the perception-action loop level. Log not just the outcomes (runs, wickets) but also the intermediate reasoning: what was the agent's confidence, which tool did it select, what was the latency breakdown? These logs become invaluable for debugging agent misbehavior. Many teams ship agents without adequate logging and then spend weeks trying to understand why an agent made a bizarre decision; with rich telemetry, you can replay that exact scenario, reproduce the bug, and fix it in minutes. Use structured logging that captures perception data, reasoning traces, selected actions, and actual outcomes—then correlate them to find patterns in failures.
Common Patterns & Best Practices

Three established patterns have emerged across production agentic systems: First, the "Observe-Reason-Act-Learn" loop is universally present but varies in implementation—rapid loops (milliseconds) suit real-time decisions while longer cycles (minutes/hours) suit strategic planning; the key is tuning frequency to the problem domain. Second, the "Graceful Degradation with Fallbacks" pattern recognizes that no tool is perfectly reliable, so systems maintain ranked alternatives: try the primary tool, if it times out try the backup tool, if that also fails try the cached result or human escalation; this prevents cascading failures and keeps agents progressing. Third, "Explicit Confidence Thresholds" separate high-confidence actions (execute immediately) from low-confidence ones (request human approval or try multiple approaches in parallel); this is critical for safety because agents with low confidence in their reasoning shouldn't make irreversible decisions. Across all three patterns, the principle is the same: embrace failure as inevitable and design mechanisms to recover gracefully. Organizations that attempt to make agents foolproof (zero errors) typically end up making them useless (unable to act when faced with imperfect information). Organizations that accept errors but handle them intelligently end up with systems that are both powerful and robust. Testing agentic systems requires a different mindset than testing traditional code: you cannot unit test an agent's decision in isolation because the decision depends on the current state, which is always changing; instead, you test the entire perception-action loop with synthetic scenarios, measure how often the agent recovers from failures, and track metrics like "percentage of actions that succeed on first attempt without escalation." Scenario testing is essential: create synthetic situations representing edge cases (market crash, server down, conflicting goals) and verify that agents handle them without catastrophic failure. The industry is still establishing best practices for agentic system testing, but the consensus is moving toward "probabilistic correctness" metrics rather than "100% correctness"—an agent is good enough if it succeeds 95% of the time and recovers from failures in 99% of cases.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The three patterns are visible in how elite cricket teams like India's 2011 World Cup squad operated under Mahendra Singh Dhoni's captaincy. First, the "Observe-Reason-Act-Learn" loop: Dhoni constantly observed how the pitch was behaving, reasoned about optimal bowling changes, acted by calling bowlers up or repositioning fields, and learned—after each over he adjusted based on what worked. Gautam Gambhir's batting during the final match showed this too: he perceived Steyn bowling short, reasoned that this was a weakness he could exploit, acted by pulling the short balls, and learned that Steyn was tiring. Second, the "Graceful Degradation" pattern: when Zaheer Khan was injured during the tournament, India didn't collapse; instead they promoted backup bowlers, adjusted field placements, and accepted slightly lower control rather than trying to replace a world-class bowler with someone equally good (impossible). They had fallbacks and ranked alternatives ready. Third, "Explicit Confidence Thresholds": Dhoni didn't take audacious risks like spinning on day 1 of a Test when he was uncertain about pitch behavior; instead, he played conservatively, built confidence through observation, then increased aggression once he understood conditions. He knew when to be confident (last over of a T20 needing 2 runs from 1 ball) and when to request help (bringing attacking fielders when uncertain about where the batter would hit). The India 2011 team was remarkable not because they never failed, but because they handled failures gracefully—when something didn't work, they pivoted quickly rather than persisting stubbornly. This is the pattern that production agentic systems should emulate: embrace uncertainty, maintain fallbacks, adjust confidence based on situation, and pivot when initial approaches fail. Teams that try to play perfect cricket (never take a chance, always play safe) become predictable and lose; teams that balance risk with recovery mechanisms win.
